q: The outermost shell electrons of atoms are the basis for understanding the joining of atoms.
The principles of filling electrons in the atomic orbitals include the Aufbau principle, Pauli's exclusion principle, and Hund's rule.
The $s$ subshell has three dumbbell-shaped orbitals known as $p_x$, $p_y$, and $p_z$.
According to the Aufbau principle, electrons are filled in the higher energy atomic orbitals before filling lower energy ones.
Aufbau is a German word that means building up.
An electron can spin in only two directions, represented with arrows as $\uparrow$ or $\downarrow$.
Pauli's exclusion principle states that two electrons with the same spin can occupy the same orbital.
Hund's rule states that every orbital in a subshell is singly occupied with one electron before any one orbital is doubly occupied.
Valence electrons are the electrons in the outermost shells of an atom.
Whenever a chemical bond forms, atoms lose, gain, or share electrons in order to achieve the stable electronic structures of noble gases.
The octet rule applies strictly to all elements, including $d$-block elements.
Lewis symbols for elements represent valence electrons as dots.
Electronegativity ($EN$) is a measure of the ability of an atom to attract the shared electrons to itself.
Electron affinity ($EA$) is the amount of energy released when an electron is added to a gaseous atom.
Ionisation energy ($IE$) is the amount of energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom.
Elements having high electron affinity easily lose electrons, resulting in the formation of cations.
Generally, the higher the first ionisation energy of an element, the greater is its electronegativity.
The strength of sharing or transferring valence electrons depends on the electronegativity of the atoms involved in the chemical bonding.
Forces between molecules, such as hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces, are known as strong forces.
London dispersion forces are the weakest intermolecular forces that can be found in all substances.
Ionic bonding results from the transfer of electrons from an atom having small ionisation energy to an atom having a large electron affinity.
Mostly ionic bonding takes place when the difference in electronegativity between two atoms is less than $1.8$.
In ionic bonding, a metal loses electrons to form positively charged ions called cations.
The number of lost or gained electrons by an atom during ionic bonding is the same as the valency of that element.
Ionic compounds are always neutral because electrons loss always equals electrons gain in an electron transfer process.
In ionic compounds, the cations and anions are held together by strong electrostatic attraction.
The melting points of ionic compounds are higher when the charge on the ions is greater.
In solid ionic compounds, ions can move freely and hence they conduct electricity.
The crystalline structure of an ionic solid is called a crystal lattice.
The cubic unit cell has three types: simple cubic ($sc$), face-centred cubic ($fcc$), and body-centred cubic ($bcc$).
Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) contains only one $Na^{+}$ ion and one $Cl^{-}$ ion in its structure.
The term coordination number is used to express the number of ions surrounding the central ion in the lattice.
Unit cell is the smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice that shows the three-dimensional pattern of the entire crystal.
A face-centred cubic unit cell is a cubic cell with an atom on each corner and an atom at the centre of the cube.
In the case of magnesium oxide ($MgO$), the electronegativity difference is $2.3$.
Electrostatic attraction is the attraction between a negatively charged ion and a positively charged ion.
Sodium fluoride can be used in water fluoridation.
Potassium iodide is added to 'iodised' salt for thyroid health.
Metal salts such as strontium carbonate and calcium chloride are used in fireworks.
Sodium, calcium, potassium, chloride, phosphate, and magnesium ions are all electrolytes.
Covalent bonding can occur between similar or even identical atoms.
A covalent bond is the electrostatic attraction between the nuclei of the atoms and the shared electrons.
In covalent bonding, paired valence electrons in each of the bonding atoms are shared.
Covalent bonds can be single bonds, double bonds, or triple bonds.
A triple bond is formed by sharing two pairs of electrons between two atoms.
The higher the number of bonded electrons, the stronger the attraction between the two atoms and the shorter the bond length.
The bond strength of the single bond is the highest among covalent bonds.
A coordinate bond is characterized by the fact that the two shared electrons are both donated by one of the participating atoms.
A coordinate bond is expressed as an arrow, $\rightarrow$, which indicates the direction in which the electrons are donated.
Molecules with incomplete octets are called electron-deficient molecules.
In carbon monoxide, the $CO$ molecule has a triple bond that consists of two covalent bonds and one coordinate bond.
Lewis structures are three-dimensional representations of how valence shell electrons are arranged.
Expanded valence shell molecules occur when the central atom has valence electrons of more than $8$.
The Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion ($VSEPR$) theory is used to predict the shape of covalent molecules.
Repulsive interactions of electron pairs decrease in the order: lone pair-lone pair $>$ lone pair-bonding pair $>$ bonding pair-bonding pair.
Lone pairs of electrons in a molecule occupy more space compared with the bonding pairs of electrons.
All diatomic molecules are linear.
A triatomic molecule of $AX_2$ type always has a bent shape.
In diamond, each carbon atom is joined to four other carbon atoms by strong covalent bonds.
Graphite has free-moving electrons in each layer, allowing it to conduct electricity.
Fullerene ($C_{60}$), carbon nanotubes, and graphene exist as giant structures.
Silicon(IV) oxide ($SiO_2$) is an example of a giant covalent molecule with a structure similar to diamond.
Bond angle is an angle between any two bonds that include a common atom.
A giant structure is a three-dimensional structure of atoms that are joined by a network of bonds.
In $PF_5$, the central phosphorus atom has $10$ electrons around it, making it an expanded valence shell molecule.
The bond angle of $F-B-F$ in $BF_3$ is $120^{\circ}$.
The molecular shape of $NH_3$ is trigonal pyramidal.
In the $VSEPR$ model, multiple bonds (double and triple bonds) behave as a single unit.
The bond angle of $H-N-H$ in $NH_3$ will be less than $109.5^{\circ}$.
Molecules such as $NO$ and $BeCl_2$ are exceptions to the octet rule.
Intermolecular forces are responsible for most of the physical and chemical properties of matter.
If two non-metal atoms in a covalent bond are not identical, the electrons will be equally shared.
Bond polarity within a molecule results in a separation of charge over the molecule.
A dipole moment of a molecule can be expressed numerically by taking into account the magnitude of the charge and the distance separating the charges.
The $\delta^{+}$ or $\delta^{-}$ signifies a partial charge that is greater in magnitude than the charge associated with a free proton or electron.
All diatomic molecules containing polar covalent bonds are polar molecules.
The dipole moment of the $CO_2$ molecule is zero because its two equal bond dipoles cancel each other.
Van der Waals forces include dipole-dipole interaction, ion-dipole interaction, and London dispersion forces.
Dipole-dipole interactions are formed when two dipolar molecules interact with each other in space.
Ion-dipole interaction happens especially in solutions where a negative ion is attracted to the positive pole of a polar molecule.
London dispersion forces are the strongest intermolecular forces.
An atom can develop a temporary dipole when the electrons are distributed unsymmetrically about the nucleus.
London dispersion forces are instantaneous dipole-induced dipole interactions that exist between all atoms and molecules.
Hydrogen bonding requires the molecule to have hydrogen attached to a small electronegative atom such as $F, O,$ or $N$.
A hydrogen bond is a special type of dipole-dipole interaction that is weaker than van der Waals forces.
The hydrogen bond is stronger than a covalent bond.
Polarity is a separation of charge, leading to one part of a bond or molecule becoming positively charged and the other negatively charged.
Non-polar molecules are molecules that have an equal distribution of charge.
Hydrogen bonding can occur as both intermolecular and intramolecular forces.
Dry ice (solid $CO_2$) is used in cold grinding and freeze drying processes.
In a metallic bond, the atoms share or transfer electrons to bond together.
In metallic solids, valence electrons are very loosely held, forming a common electron cloud.
Mobile electrons in metals are described as a 'sea of electrons'.
Metallic bonding results from the interaction of mobile electrons with the various nuclei.
The strength of metallic bonding increases with the increasing positive charge on the ions in the metal lattice.
The strength of metallic bonding increases with the increasing size of metal ions in the lattice.
The strength of metallic bonding increases with the increasing number of mobile electrons per atom.
The strength of metallic bond is generally higher than that of an ionic bond.
Metals consist of giant structures of atoms packed closely together in a regular arrangement called a lattice.
The melting points of some metals become lower compared with ionic compounds.
